prompt:
Ticket from the museum collections team. Registrars edit accession records in long sessions and the UI needs to know what actually changed before it writes anything back to the catalog — and they need a working "discard my edits" button. We're doing this as a tiny model layer, no gems.

Create `dirty_model.rb` with a `DirtyModel` base class. A model declares its columns like this:

    class ArtifactRecord < DirtyModel
      attributes :title, :era, :insured_value, :on_display
    end

That one macro line has to produce readers and writers for exactly those attributes. Generate them however you like — define_method in the macro, or method_missing — but introspection must tell the truth either way: `respond_to?(:title)` and `respond_to?(:title=)` are true, `respond_to?(:weight_kg)` is false, and `record.method(:era)` returns a callable Method object. Reading or writing an attribute that was never declared raises NoMethodError, and each subclass keeps its own list (LoanRecord's columns must not bleed into ArtifactRecord).

Construction takes a hash of initial values: `ArtifactRecord.new(title: "Bronze Bell", era: "Ming")`. Declared-but-omitted attributes read as nil. An undeclared key in the hash is ArgumentError "unknown attribute :weight_kg" (inspect form of the key).

Dirty tracking, the ActiveModel flavor:
- a fresh record is clean: `changed?` is false, `changes` is {}
- after `rec.era = "Qing"`, `changes` is { era: ["Ming", "Qing"] } — [what it was constructed with, what it is now]
- edit the same attribute twice and changes still holds the ORIGINAL value paired with the LATEST: assign 15_000 then 18_000 over an original 12_000 and you get [12_000, 18_000]
- assigning a value equal to the original CLEARS that entry — set it back and the record is clean again; assigning the current value is not a change at all
- nil originals track like anything else: { era: [nil, "Han"] }
- `changes` lists attributes in DECLARATION order no matter what order the edits happened in, and it returns a defensive copy — callers scribbling on the returned hash or its [old, new] pairs must not corrupt the record
- `rollback!` restores every original value, leaves the record clean, returns self, and is a quiet no-op on a clean record; tracking keeps working normally after a rollback
- instances are independent: two records of the same class never share dirty state

`ruby test_dirty_model.rb` is the acceptance gate (the test file defines its own ArtifactRecord and LoanRecord subclasses, so the macro is exercised exactly as written above). The tests are hands-off.
